title: Project Risk Analysis
text: simple example use package common PRA task. First, load package: Suppose simple project 3 tasks (, B, C), duration task uncertain. can describe uncertainty task using probability distributions run Monte Carlo Simulation (MCS) estimate overall project duration. , set number simulations describe probability distributions work package. case, run 10,000 simulations following distributions: run simulation using mcs function store results: visualize results, can create histogram total project duration. can also overlay normal distribution curve based mean standard deviation results:  give visual representation uncertainty total project duration based individual task distributions. average, project expected take around 30 time units, take little 20 much 40 time units.
code: library(PRA) num_simulations <- 10000 task_distributions <- list(   list(type = "normal", mean = 10, sd = 2), # Task A: Normal distribution   list(type = "triangular", a = 5, b = 10, c = 15), # Task B: Triangular distribution   list(type = "uniform", min = 8, max = 12) # Task C: Uniform distribution ) results <- mcs(num_simulations, task_distributions) hist(results$total_distribution,   freq = FALSE, breaks = 50, main = "Distribution of Total Project Duration",   xlab = "Total Duration", col = "skyblue", border = "white" ) curve(dnorm(x, mean = results$total_mean, sd = results$total_sd), add = TRUE, col = "darkblue")

title: Summary
text: PRA R package quantitative project risk management. provides unified, open-source implementation core analytical methods used cost engineering schedule risk analysis: Monte Carlo simulation (MCS), Second Moment Method (SMM), Earned Value Management (EVM), contingency analysis, sensitivity analysis, Bayesian risk inference, sigmoidal learning curves, Design Structure Matrices (DSM). package available CRAN targets practitioners researchers working project risk, cost engineering, construction management. Source code documentation available https://github.com/paulgovan/PRA.

title: Summary
text: Quantitative risk analysis projects requires integrating several methodologically distinct techniques — probabilistic simulation, performance measurement, Bayesian updating, dependency modeling — typically scattered across commercial tools, spreadsheets, single-purpose scripts. prior R package provides cohesive, peer-reviewed implementation full suite. PRA addresses gap consolidating methods minimal, well-tested package adheres rOpenSci Statistical Software Review (SRR) standards [@rOpenSciStats2021], enabling reproducible analyses directly R. package particularly useful early project phases uncertainty high formal risk quantification can guide contingency budgeting schedule buffering decisions [@Damnjanovic2020].

title: Summary
text: Commercial tools Palisade @RISK (Oracle) Primavera Risk Analysis widely used project risk simulation proprietary, expensive, reproducible open scientific workflows. Within R, single package covers full project risk analysis workflow. mc2d package provides general-purpose two-dimensional Monte Carlo simulation project-specific constructs (task networks, EVM, contingency). sensitivity package implements variance-based sensitivity indices general functions integrate schedule cost models. EVM metrics absent CRAN entirely. Bayesian inference packages rstan brms operate much higher level generality require substantial user expertise apply project risk contexts. PRA fills gap providing domain-specific, batteries-included toolkit covers complete analytical lifecycle initial uncertainty estimation performance monitoring Bayesian updating, minimal dependencies consistent API.

title: Summary
text: mcs() function propagates schedule cost uncertainty project network using triangular, normal, uniform distributions. Task correlations modeled via Cholesky decomposition [@Vose2008]. Results include full empirical distributions standard percentile summaries (P5–P95).

title: Summary
text: smm() provides analytical alternative simulation: approximates project-level mean variance task-level first second moments, invoking Central Limit Theorem. useful rapid early-stage estimates full distributional assumptions yet justified [@Damnjanovic2020].

title: Summary
text: EVM module implements complete ANSI/EIA-748 suite eleven performance metrics: Planned Value (PV), Earned Value (EV), Actual Cost (AC), Schedule Variance (SV), Cost Variance (CV), Schedule Performance Index (SPI), Cost Performance Index (CPI), Estimate Completion (EAC, three methods), Estimate Complete (ETC), -Complete Performance Index (TCPI), Variance Completion (VAC) [@Fleming2010].

title: Summary
text: contingency() derives cost schedule reserve buffers difference user-specified simulation percentile (e.g., P80 P95) base estimate. sensitivity() decomposes total project variance task contribution, enabling Tornado-style identification dominant risk drivers [@Damnjanovic2020].

title: Summary
text: risk_prob() computes overall risk event probability root-cause priors conditional likelihoods using total probability theorem. risk_post_prob() updates probabilities observed evidence via Bayes’ theorem. conjugate Beta-Binomial model cost_pdf() cost_post_pdf() extends framework cost risk distributions [@Gelman2013].

title: Summary
text: fit_sigmoidal() fits three S-curve models (Logistic, Gompertz, Pearl) cumulative completion data using nonlinear least squares via minpack.lm [@Elzhov2016]. predict_sigmoidal() forecasts future task completion dates, plot_sigmoidal() provides visualization.

title: Summary
text: parent_dsm() grandparent_dsm() construct resource-task risk-resource-task dependency matrices, respectively. support identification task coupling risk propagation paths complex project networks [@Browning2001].

title: Summary
text: PRA v0.4.0 includes agentic interface (pra_chat(), pra_app()) exposes analytical functions tools callable large language models via ellmer package [@ellmer]. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) layer, built ragnar [@ragnar] local Ollama models, grounds agent responses domain-specific risk literature. package also exports pra_mcp_server() serve tools via Model Context Protocol [@MCP2024], enabling integration Claude, Claude Code, MCP-compatible AI clients.

title: Summary
text: PRA structured collection independent analytical modules, source file R/, corresponding test file tests/testthat/. one-module-per-file layout keeps individual files small, makes dependency graph explicit, allows modules tested isolation. public functions follow consistent input-validation-first pattern: inputs checked correct type, length, absence NA, NaN, Inf values computation occurs, following SRR standards G2.0–G2.16. Error messages unique per call site (SRR G5.2a) aid debugging. AI agent layer (R/chat.R, R/tools.R, R/rag.R) kept strictly optional via Suggests mechanism: 36 exported analytical functions work without AI dependency. AI features used, tool definitions built ellmer::tool() reused identically conversational chat interface MCP server, avoiding duplication. RAG store uses DuckDB via ragnar, enabling hybrid vector keyword search bundled domain knowledge files without requiring network connection. package uses minimal hard dependencies (mc2d, minpack.lm, stats) reduce installation friction downstream breakage risk.

title: Summary
text: 36 exported functions validate inputs type, length, special values (NA, NaN, Inf) following rOpenSci SRR standards G2.0–G2.16. test suite contains 640 test cases 1,200 assertions covering error/warning behavior, parameter recovery, edge conditions, noise susceptibility, algorithmic correctness, run via testthat [@testthat] verified GitHub Actions CI macOS, Windows, Ubuntu across R release, devel, oldrel. Code coverage tracked via Codecov.

title: Summary
text: AI agent interface within PRA uses large language models runtime optional feature: pra_chat() connects locally-running Ollama model (default: llama3.2) user-supplied ellmer chat object, pra_mcp_server() exposes PRA tools external AI clients via Model Context Protocol. deliberate, documented design choices software , uses generative AI development process. development, Claude Code (Anthropic) used assist drafting documentation, generating test scaffolding, reviewing code. substantive design decisions, algorithmic implementations, final content authored verified package maintainer.

title: PRA: Project Risk Analysis — PRA-package
text: Monte Carlo Simulation (mcs()) Propagates schedule cost uncertainty triangular distribution model using mc2d. Returns S3 object print method. Second Moment Method (smm()) Analytical first- second-moment propagation uncertainty without simulation. Returns S3 object print method. Earned Value Management (pv(), ev(), ac(), sv(), cv(), spi(), cpi(), eac(), etc(), tcpi(), vac()) Full suite EVM performance metrics forecasting functions. Contingency Analysis (contingency()) Derives cost schedule contingency simulation output user-specified confidence level. Sensitivity Analysis (sensitivity()) Ranks risk drivers contribution overall project uncertainty. Learning Curves (fit_sigmoidal(), predict_sigmoidal(), plot_sigmoidal()) Fits Pearl, Gompertz, Logistic sigmoidal growth models cumulative cost progress data using nonlinear least squares (minpack.lm). Bayesian Risk Inference (risk_prob(), risk_post_prob()) Beta-Binomial conjugate updating risk event probabilities. Correlation Matrices (cor_matrix()) Constructs valid positive-definite correlation matrices use multivariate simulations. Design Structure Matrix (parent_dsm(), grandparent_dsm()) Derives direct (parent) indirect (grandparent) dependency structure matrices binary adjacency matrix.

text: Damnjanovic, Ivan, Kenneth Reinschmidt. Data analytics engineering construction project risk management. . 172534. Cham, Switzerland: Springer, 2020.

title: Contingency Calculation. — contingency
code: # Set the number os simulations and the task distributions for a toy project. num_sims <- 10000 task_dists <- list(   list(type = "normal", mean = 10, sd = 2), # Task A: Normal distribution   list(type = "triangular", a = 5, b = 10, c = 15), # Task B: Triangular distribution   list(type = "uniform", min = 8, max = 12) # Task C: Uniform distribution )  # Set the correlation matrix for the correlations between tasks. cor_mat <- matrix(c(   1, 0.5, 0.3,   0.5, 1, 0.4,   0.3, 0.4, 1 ), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)  # Run the Monte Carlo simulation. results <- mcs(num_sims, task_dists, cor_mat)  # Calculate the contingency and print the results. contingency <- contingency(results, phigh = 0.95, pbase = 0.50) cat("Contingency based on 95th percentile and 50th percentile:", contingency) #> Contingency based on 95th percentile and 50th percentile: 7.214802  # Without correlation matrix results_indep <- mcs(num_sims, task_dists) contingency_indep <- contingency(results_indep,   phigh = 0.95,   pbase = 0.50 ) cat("Contingency based on 95th percentile and 50th percentile ( independent tasks):", contingency_indep) #> Contingency based on 95th percentile and 50th percentile ( #> independent tasks): 5.220138  # Build a barplot to visualize the contingency results. contingency_data <- data.frame(   Scenario = c("With Correlation", "Independent Tasks"),   Contingency = c(contingency, contingency_indep) ) barplot(   height = contingency_data$Contingency,   names = contingency_data$Scenario,   col = c("orange", "purple"),   horiz = TRUE,   xlab = "Contingency",   ylab = "Scenario" ) title("Contingency Calculation for Project Scenarios")

code: eac(bac, method = "typical", cpi = NULL, ac = NULL, ev = NULL, spi = NULL)

title: Estimate at Completion (EAC). — eac
text: bac Budget Completion (BAC) (total planned budget). method EAC calculation method. One : "typical" (default): EAC = BAC / CPI. Assumes future work performed current cost efficiency. "atypical": EAC = AC + (BAC - EV). Assumes future work performed planned rate. "combined": EAC = AC + (BAC - EV) / (CPI * SPI). Considers cost schedule performance. cpi Cost Performance Index (CPI). Required "typical" "combined" methods. ac Actual Cost. Required "atypical" "combined" methods. ev Earned Value. Required "atypical" "combined" methods. spi Schedule Performance Index. Required "combined" method.

") #> EAC (typical): 120481.9   # Method 2: Atypical - assumes future work at planned rate ac <- 63000 ev <- 35000 eac <- eac(bac, method = "atypical", ac = ac, ev = ev) cat("EAC (atypical):", round(eac, 2), "
") #> EAC (atypical): 128000   # Method 3: Combined - considers both CPI and SPI spi <- 0.875 eac <- eac(bac, method = "combined", cpi = cpi, ac = ac, ev = ev, spi = spi) cat("EAC (combined):", round(eac, 2), "
") #> EAC (combined): 152500.9

title: Monte Carlo Simulation. — mcs
text: num_sims number simulations run. task_dists list lists describing task distribution parameters. task distribution specified list "type" field (indicating distribution type: "normal", "triangular", "uniform") corresponding parameters: "normal" (mean, sd), "triangular" (, b, c), "uniform" (min, max). example: list( list(type = "normal", mean = 10, sd = 2), list(type = "triangular", = 5, b = 10, c = 15), list(type = "uniform", min = 8, max = 12) ) cor_mat correlation matrix tasks (Optional). provided, tasks assumed independent.

title: Monte Carlo Simulation. — mcs
text: function returns list total mean, variance, standard deviation, percentiles project.

title: Monte Carlo Simulation. — mcs
text: Damnjanovic, Ivan, Kenneth Reinschmidt. Data analytics engineering construction project risk management. . 172534. Cham, Switzerland: Springer, 2020.

title: Monte Carlo Simulation. — mcs
code:
# Set the number of simulations and task distributions for a toy project. num_sims <- 10000 task_dists <- list(   list(type = "normal", mean = 10, sd = 2), # Task A: Normal distribution   list(type = "triangular", a = 5, b = 10, c = 15), # Task B: Triangular distribution   list(type = "uniform", min = 8, max = 12) # Task C: Uniform distribution )  # Set the correlation matrix for the correlations between tasks. cor_mat <- matrix(c(   1, 0.5, 0.3,   0.5, 1, 0.4,   0.3, 0.4, 1 ), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)  # Run the Monte Carlo sumulation and print the results. results <- mcs(num_sims, task_dists, cor_mat) cat("Mean Total Duration:", results$total_mean, "
") #> Mean Total Duration: 38.61296  cat("Variance of Total Variance:", results$total_variance, "
") #> Variance of Total Variance: 19.89063  cat("Standard Deviation of Total Duration:", results$total_sd, "
") #> Standard Deviation of Total Duration: 4.459891  cat("5th Percentile:", results$percentiles[1], "
") #> 5th Percentile: 31.20758  cat("Median (50th Percentile):", results$percentiles[2], "
") #> Median (50th Percentile): 38.63446  cat("95th Percentile:", results$percentiles[3], "
") #> 95th Percentile: 46.00121  hist(results$total_distribution,   breaks = 50, main = "Distribution of Total Project Duration",   xlab = "Total Duration", col = "skyblue", border = "white" ) legend("topright", legend = c("Total Duration Distribution"), fill = c("skyblue"))

title: Perform Bayesian Learning on a Probabilistic Network of Project Risks. — prob_net_learn
text: function updates probabilistic network project risks observed values certain nodes performs inference generate posterior distributions unobserved nodes. function supports normal, uniform, lognormal, conditional continuous, conditional discrete, discrete, aggregate (summation) node types. Normal nodes sampled normal distribution using specified mean sd. Uniform nodes sampled uniform distribution specified min max values. Lognormal nodes sampled lognormal distribution specified meanlog sdlog. Conditional nodes depend discrete conditional node; condition TRUE (value = 1), node follows true_dist, otherwise follows false_dist (value = 0). Conditional distributions can normal, lognormal, uniform, discrete. Discrete nodes sampled using sample(), aggregate nodes computed sum values specified nodes. Observed nodes fixed given values.

title: Perform Bayesian Learning on a Probabilistic Network of Project Risks. — prob_net_learn
code: # Define nodes nodes <- data.frame(   id = c("A", "B", "C", "D"),   label = c("Node A", "Node B", "Node C", "Node D"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Define links links <- data.frame(   source = c("A", "A", "B", "C"),   target = c("B", "C", "D", "D"),   weight = c(1, 2, 3, 4),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Define distributions for nodes distributions <- list(   A = list(type = "discrete", values = c(0, 1), probs = c(0.5, 0.5)),   B = list(type = "normal", mean = 2, sd = 0.5),   C = list(     type = "conditional", condition = "A",     true_dist = list(type = "normal", mean = 1, sd = 0.5),     false_dist = list(type = "discrete", values = c(0, 1), probs = c(0.4, 0.6))   ),   D = list(type = "aggregate", nodes = c("B", "C")) )  # Create the network graph graph <- prob_net(nodes, links, distributions = distributions)  # Perform Bayesian updating with observations observations <- list(A = 1) updated_results <- prob_net_learn(graph, observations, num_samples = 1000) head(updated_results) #>   A        B         C        D #> 1 1 2.129639 1.7865548 3.916194 #> 2 1 2.345738 1.1611199 3.506858 #> 3 1 1.817078 0.8336464 2.650725 #> 4 1 2.601603 0.2117512 2.813354 #> 5 1 1.503451 0.4629828 1.966434 #> 6 1 2.595935 1.1311792 3.727114

code: # Define nodes nodes <- data.frame(   id = c("A", "B", "C", "D"),   label = c("Node A", "Node B", "Node C", "Node D"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Define links links <- data.frame(   source = c("A", "A", "B", "C"),   target = c("B", "C", "D", "D"),   weight = c(1, 2, 3, 4),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Define distributions for nodes distributions <- list(   A = list(type = "discrete", values = c(0, 1), probs = c(0.5, 0.5)),   B = list(type = "normal", mean = 2, sd = 0.5),   C = list(     type = "conditional", condition = "A",     true_dist = list(type = "normal", mean = 1, sd = 0.5),     false_dist = list(type = "lognormal", meanlog = 0, sdlog = 0.2)   ),   D = list(type = "aggregate", nodes = c("B", "C")) )  # Create the network graph graph <- prob_net(nodes, links, distributions = distributions)  # Perform inference (simulate 1000 samples) simulation_results <- prob_net_sim(graph, num_samples = 1000) head(simulation_results) #>   A        B         C        D #> 1 1 2.500190 1.4038386 3.904028 #> 2 0 1.712608 0.9651617 2.677770 #> 3 0 1.819046 0.7255989 2.544645 #> 4 1 2.849409 0.6168564 3.466266 #> 5 0 2.206482 1.0417206 3.248202 #> 6 0 2.344146 1.0655631 3.409709

title: Sensitivity Analysis. — sensitivity
text: Damnjanovic, Ivan, Kenneth Reinschmidt. Data analytics engineering construction project risk management. . 172534. Cham, Switzerland: Springer, 2020.

title: Sensitivity Analysis. — sensitivity
code: # Set the task distributions for a toy project. task_dists <- list(   list(type = "normal", mean = 10, sd = 2), # Task A: Normal distribution   list(type = "triangular", a = 5, b = 15, c = 10), # Task B: Triangular distribution   list(type = "uniform", min = 8, max = 12) # Task C: Uniform distribution )  # Set the correlation matrix between the tasks. cor_mat <- matrix(c(   1, 0.5, 0.3,   0.5, 1, 0.4,   0.3, 0.4, 1 ), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)  # Calculate the sensitivity of each task and print the results sensitivity_results <- sensitivity(task_dists, cor_mat) print(sensitivity_results) #> [1] 2.6 2.8 2.4  # Build a vertical barchart and display the results. data <- data.frame(   Tasks = c("A", "B", "C"),   Sensitivity = sensitivity_results ) barplot(   height = data$Sensitivity, names = data$Tasks, col = "skyblue",   horiz = TRUE, xlab = "Sensitivity", ylab = "Tasks" ) title("Sensitivity Analysis of Project Tasks")   # Without correlation matrix sensitivity_results_indep <- sensitivity(task_dists) print(sensitivity_results_indep) #> [1] 1 1 1  # Build a vertical barchart and display the results. data_indep <- data.frame(   Tasks = c("A", "B", "C"),   Sensitivity = sensitivity_results_indep ) barplot(   height = data_indep$Sensitivity, names = data_indep$Tasks,   col = "lightgreen",   horiz = TRUE, xlab = "Sensitivity", ylab = "Tasks" ) title("Sensitivity Analysis of Project Tasks (Independent)")

title: Second Moment Method Analysis. — smm
text: function performs Second Moment Method (SMM) analysis estimate total mean, variance, standard deviation project based individual task means, variances, optional correlation matrix.

title: Second Moment Method Analysis. — smm
code: smm(mean, var, cor_mat = NULL)

title: Second Moment Method Analysis. — smm
text: mean mean vector. var variance vector. cor_mat correlation matrix (optional). provided, tasks assumed independent.

title: Second Moment Method Analysis. — smm
text: function returns list total mean, variance, standard deviation project.

title: Second Moment Method Analysis. — smm
text: Damnjanovic, Ivan, Kenneth Reinschmidt. Data analytics engineering construction project risk management. . 172534. Cham, Switzerland: Springer, 2020.

title: Second Moment Method Analysis. — smm
code: # Set the mean vector, variance vector, and correlation matrix for a toy project. mean <- c(10, 15, 20) var <- c(4, 9, 16) cor_mat <- matrix(c(   1, 0.5, 0.3,   0.5, 1, 0.4,   0.3, 0.4, 1 ), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)  # Use the Second Moment Method to estimate the results for the project. result <- smm(mean, var, cor_mat) print(result) #> Second Moment Method Results: #> ------------------------------ #> Total Mean:  45  #> Total Variance:  49.4  #> Total Standard Deviation:  7.028513   # Without correlation matrix (independent tasks) result <- smm(mean, var) print(result) #> Second Moment Method Results: #> ------------------------------ #> Total Mean:  45  #> Total Variance:  29  #> Total Standard Deviation:  5.385165   # When certain tasks are discrete and others are continuous, the SMM can still # be applied as long as the variance values accurately reflect the variability of each task.  discrete_mean <- c(5, 10) discrete_var <- c(0, 0) continuous_mean <- c(15, 20) continuous_var <- c(4, 5) mean <- c(discrete_mean, continuous_mean) var <- c(discrete_var, continuous_var) cor_mat <- matrix(c(   1, 0, 0.2, 0.3,   0, 1, 0.1, 0.2,   0.2, 0.1, 1, 0.4,   0.3, 0.2, 0.4,   1 ), nrow = 4, byrow = TRUE) result <- smm(mean, var, cor_mat) print(result) #> Second Moment Method Results: #> ------------------------------ #> Total Mean:  50  #> Total Variance:  12.57771  #> Total Standard Deviation:  3.546507

title: Schedule Performance Index (SPI). — spi

title: Schedule Performance Index (SPI). — spi

title: Schedule Performance Index (SPI). — spi
text: ev Earned Value. pv Planned Value.

title: Schedule Performance Index (SPI). — spi
text: function returns Schedule Performance Index (SPI) work completed.

title: Schedule Performance Index (SPI). — spi
text: Damnjanovic, Ivan, Kenneth Reinschmidt. Data analytics engineering construction project risk management. . 172534. Cham, Switzerland: Springer, 2020.

title: Schedule Performance Index (SPI). — spi
code: # Set the BAC, schedule, and current time period for an example project. bac <- 100000 schedule <- c(0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.7, 1.0) time_period <- 3  # Calculate the PV. pv <- pv(bac, schedule, time_period)  # Set the actual % complete and calculate the EV. actual_per_complete <- 0.35 ev <- ev(bac, actual_per_complete)  # Calculate the SPI and print the results. spi <- spi(ev, pv) cat("Schedule Performance Index (SPI):", spi, "\n") #> Schedule Performance Index (SPI): 0.875

title: To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI). — tcpi
text: Calculates -Complete Performance Index (TCPI), indicates cost performance required remaining work meet target (BAC EAC). TCPI > 1 means efficiency must improve; TCPI < 1 means efficiency can decrease.

title: To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI). — tcpi
code: tcpi(bac, ev, ac, target = "bac", eac = NULL)

title: To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI). — tcpi
text: bac Budget Completion (BAC) (total planned budget). ev Earned Value. ac Actual Cost. target target calculate TCPI . Either "bac" (default) meet original budget, "eac" meet revised estimate. "eac", eac parameter must provided. eac Estimate Completion. Required target = "eac".

title: To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI). — tcpi
text: function returns -Complete Performance Index (TCPI).

title: To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI). — tcpi
text: Damnjanovic, Ivan, Kenneth Reinschmidt. Data analytics engineering construction project risk management. . 172534. Cham, Switzerland: Springer, 2020.

title: To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI). — tcpi
code: bac <- 100000 ev <- 35000 ac <- 63000  # TCPI to complete within original budget tcpi_bac <- tcpi(bac, ev, ac) cat("TCPI (to meet BAC):", round(tcpi_bac, 2), "\n") #> TCPI (to meet BAC): 1.76   # TCPI to complete within revised estimate eac <- 120482 tcpi_eac <- tcpi(bac, ev, ac, target = "eac", eac = eac) cat("TCPI (to meet EAC):", round(tcpi_eac, 2), "\n") #> TCPI (to meet EAC): 1.13

text: Damnjanovic, Ivan, Kenneth Reinschmidt. Data analytics engineering construction project risk management. . 172534. Cham, Switzerland: Springer, 2020.

title: Variance at Completion (VAC). — vac
code: bac <- 100000 eac <- 120482 # From EAC calculation  vac <- vac(bac, eac) cat("Variance at Completion (VAC):", round(vac, 2), "\n") #> Variance at Completion (VAC): -20482  cat("Project is expected to be", abs(round(vac, 2)), ifelse(vac < 0, "over", "under"), "budget\n") #> Project is expected to be 20482 over budget
